metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an assertion in `test_release_workflow_pins.py` that the `mcp_registry` job contains a step with `chmod +x mcp-publisher` (or equivalent) after the `actions/download-artifact` step, so the exec-bit restoration is statically verified alongside the permission-structure check.
findings.finding: The `test_release_workflow_pins.py` static checks do not assert that `chmod +x mcp-publisher` is present in the `mcp_registry` job, yet the plan itself identifies its absence as a runtime 'Permission denied' failure. The AC states 'mcp_registry must chmod +x mcp-publisher after download-artifact' but no proving command covers this — an implementer who omits the step would pass all tests and hit a silent runtime breakage.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5 (MCP publisher split jobs); What section — 'Restore the executable bit after the handoff'
findings.suggested_fix: In the test's job-permission-structure check (assertion (e)), also parse the `mcp_registry` job's steps list and assert that at least one step's `run` field matches `chmod +x mcp-publisher` and that it appears after the download-artifact step. Example: `assert any('chmod +x mcp-publisher' in (s.get('run') or '') for s in mcp_registry_steps), 'mcp_registry missing chmod +x mcp-publisher'`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add assertion (g) to `test_release_workflow_pins.py`: parse the `mcp_registry` job steps in `release.yml` and assert that a `chmod +x mcp-publisher` step appears after the `actions/download-artifact` step, failing if it is absent.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly identifies 'chmod +x mcp-publisher after download-artifact' as a must-have runtime correctness requirement ("or the ./mcp-publisher login call fails 'Permission denied'") but omits a static assertion for it from test_release_workflow_pins.py's enumerated check list (checks a–f), leaving this failure mode undetectable under `make test`.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — MCP publisher AC bullet (chmod +x callout) and Scope — tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py check list (a–f)
findings.suggested_fix: In the `test_release_workflow_pins.py` spec, append check (g): after locating the download-artifact step in mcp_registry, assert the next step (or any subsequent step before the first `./mcp-publisher` invocation) contains `chmod +x mcp-publisher`. Mirror the pattern already used for checks (b) and (e) which inspect job-level YAML structure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the Scope or What section, explicitly specify mcp_verify's 'needs:' value (e.g. 'needs: publish') and assign the 'Wait for PyPI' polling step to exactly one of the two new jobs (mcp_verify or mcp_registry), so the job-chain topology is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan splits the existing mcp_registry job (release.yml lines 109-179, currently 'needs: publish') into two jobs (mcp_verify + mcp_registry), but never specifies what mcp_verify's 'needs:' dependency is, nor which of the two new jobs inherits the 'Wait for PyPI' polling step currently in mcp_registry — leaving the job-chain topology underspecified.
findings.location: What section (MCP publisher — download → verify → execute) / Scope (.github/workflows/release.yml split)
findings.suggested_fix: Assign 'Wait for PyPI' to mcp_verify (no OIDC token needed) and set 'needs: publish' on mcp_verify, with mcp_registry declaring 'needs: [mcp_verify]'. This preserves the PyPI-readiness guard before OIDC scope is activated.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend `test_release_workflow_pins.py` (or the AC) to also assert that the workflow steps contributing to each evidence fragment contain the specific commands that generate the required content — e.g., that the evidence-shapin upload step is preceded by a step whose `run` includes action SHA echoes or `pip list --format=freeze > tool-versions.txt`, and that evidence-mcp is preceded by a step echoing the verified MCP publisher digest — so content correctness is statically verifiable.
findings.finding: AC6 claims release evidence 'records every action SHA, the Python build-tool versions, the MCP publisher version + verified digest', but the proving command only checks that the three fragment names and a consolidation step appear in `release.yml` YAML — it never asserts the CONTENT of the uploaded artifacts contains the required data. An implementation could upload empty or incomplete evidence files and pass all static tests.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6 (release evidence); Scope — test_release_workflow_pins.py check (f)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between the 'unless a later git operation demonstrably needs it' exception in the What section and the unconditional test assertion in AC #2 — either remove the exception clause (if no such operation exists in this workflow) or add an escape-hatch mechanism (e.g., an allow-list comment in the workflow + a corresponding test carve-out) so both sections agree.
findings.finding: The 'What' section permits an exception to persist-credentials: false ('unless a later git operation demonstrably needs the persisted credential'), but AC #2 and its test have no exception mechanism — the test unconditionally fails if any checkout lacks the flag. An implementer who legitimately needs persisted credentials for a git push would find the plan self-contradictory: the body authorizes an exception, the AC forbids it.
findings.location: What section ('Set persist-credentials: false … unless a later git operation …') vs. AC #2 ('checks every actions/checkout block … and fails if it is absent')
findings.suggested_fix: If no step in release.yml actually requires persisted credentials, remove the 'unless' clause from the What section so the AC's unconditional assertion is the sole authoritative rule. If a step does need it, document which checkout that is and add a named exception to both the What description and the test logic.

findings.finding: The plan specifies a 'final aggregation step' that downloads all evidence fragments and concatenates them into `release-evidence.txt`, but does not name which job hosts this step or what its `needs:` dependencies and permissions should be. An executor must invent this design choice without guidance.
findings.location: What section — 'Print and retain release evidence' paragraph; Acceptance Criteria — fifth bullet (release evidence)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'A new `release_evidence` job (`needs: [build_dist, mcp_verify, mcp_registry]`, no elevated permissions) runs `actions/download-artifact` for each fragment and concatenates them: `cat evidence-shapin/tool-versions.txt evidence-build/build-info.txt evidence-mcp/mcp-info.txt > release-evidence.txt`, then uploads the consolidated artifact.'

findings.finding: The plan uses a pipe-separated grep pattern to assert fragment-name presence (`evidence-shapin|evidence-build|evidence-mcp|evidence-artifacts`) but never specifies per-name cardinality. A single line in release.yml that concatenates all four names (e.g. in a comment or a single `echo`) would satisfy the grep while the actual upload-artifact steps remain absent — a substring false-positive per VCDT defect #1/#5.
findings.location: Scope section '.github/workflows/release-lint.yml' check (f); AC6
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the pipe-or grep with four separate anchored greps: `grep -E "name:\s*evidence-shapin" .github/workflows/release.yml`, repeated for each fragment name. This ensures the names appear in the correct structural position.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Decide between `pip-compile` (pip-tools) and `uv pip compile` as the canonical lock-file generator, add whichever is chosen to `pyproject.toml [dev]`, and update the docs AC grep substring accordingly — documenting the rationale for the choice.
findings.finding: `pip-compile` (pip-tools) is introduced as a new external tool dependency for generating `.github/release-requirements.txt`, but `pip-tools` is absent from `pyproject.toml [dev]` dependencies (confirmed by inspection), and the plan itself names `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` as a directly equivalent alternative. Mandating pip-tools over `uv` (if `uv` is already in use in the project) adds a new tool to the toolchain without a documented preference rationale, constituting config-surface proliferation.
findings.location: Scope section (`.github/release-requirements.txt`); What section ('pip-compile --generate-hashes (pip-tools) (or uv pip compile --generate-hashes)'); AC3; docs AC grep check substring `pip-compile --generate-hashes`
findings.suggested_fix: If `uv` is already the project's package-management tool, standardize on `uv pip compile --generate-hashes`, add it (or confirm it) in dev dependencies, and update the docs AC grep check substring from `pip-compile --generate-hashes` to `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` (or a tool-agnostic phrase like `--generate-hashes`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the pip-tools vs. `uv pip compile` choice explicitly in the plan with a written rationale, and update the docs AC grep literal accordingly — do not leave both options open while locking in `pip-compile --generate-hashes` as the asserted substring.
findings.finding: The plan selects `pip-compile --generate-hashes` (pip-tools) as the canonical lock-file tool, baking this choice irrecoverably into a docs AC grep literal (`pip-compile --generate-hashes` must appear verbatim in `docs/releasing.md`), but provides no justification for introducing pip-tools over `uv pip compile --generate-hashes`, which the plan's own 'What' section lists first as equivalent. pip-tools is not present in the project's existing dev dependencies, CI workflows, or any other file in the repo, making it a new dependency to install, document, and maintain. The `uv` tool is already the project's self-described preferred option ('uv: full-SHA action pins … uv pip compile --generate-hashes') but is then silently superseded by pip-tools.
findings.location: What section ('pip-compile --generate-hashes (pip-tools)') / Scope: `.github/release-requirements.txt` / AC line 3 / AC line 7 (docs grep)
findings.suggested_fix: If uv is already installed in the project's environment, prefer `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` and update the docs AC grep to assert `uv pip compile --generate-hashes`. If pip-tools is chosen for a concrete reason (e.g. richer annotation, broader pip compat), state that reason explicitly and add pip-tools to pyproject.toml's dev extras so it is installed via `pip install -e '.[dev]'`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the UPDATE trigger for `.github/release-requirements.txt` — either add a Dependabot `pip` entry for `.github/` or document a scheduled manual review cadence in `docs/releasing.md` (Pinning section) so the lock file lifecycle has a clear owner and trigger.
findings.finding: The lifecycle for keeping `.github/release-requirements.txt` current is incompletely specified: the plan says to 'update pins through reviewed dependency-update changes' but does not identify who or what triggers the detection of outdated pins (no Dependabot config, no scheduled CI check, no mention of how a maintainer knows when `build`, Hatchling, or Twine have new versions), leaving the UPDATE step in the lock file's CREATE/UPDATE/CONSUME lifecycle without a defined owner or trigger.
findings.location: What section — hash-locked build environment; Scope — .github/release-requirements.txt; Out of scope note
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to `.github/dependabot.yml`:
```yaml
- package-ecosystem: pip
  directory: /.github
  schedule:
    interval: weekly
```
and document this in the Pinning section of `docs/releasing.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC #5) to cover the checksums.txt verification path: specify what 'prefer checksums.txt' means concretely (e.g., parse the upstream file, extract the matching line, verify with sha256sum -c), or collapse the description to a single approach (always hard-code at pin time) so the description and ACs agree.
findings.finding: The What section describes preferring an upstream 'checksums.txt' if modelcontextprotocol/registry ships one, but no AC covers the verification logic for that path — only the hard-coded SHA-256 fallback is tested (AC #5: 'If upstream ships no checksums, the pinned SHA-256 is hard-coded … with a rationale comment'). The checksums.txt path is described work with no covering criterion.
findings.location: What section ('Prefer an upstream checksums.txt if modelcontextprotocol/registry ships one') vs. AC #5 (covers only the hard-coded SHA-256 fallback)
findings.suggested_fix: Simplest resolution: remove the 'prefer checksums.txt' clause from What and always use hard-coded SHA-256 at pin time with a rationale comment, matching the AC as written. If the upstream-checksums path is genuinely desired, add an explicit AC asserting the workflow fetches and verifies against the upstream checksums file before extraction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `--max-time 120 --retry 3 --retry-delay 5` (or equivalent) to the `curl` invocation in `mcp_verify` and document the timeout value so operators know when to expect a fast failure on network issues.
findings.finding: The `curl` download step specifies `-fsSL` but no `--max-time` or `--connect-timeout`, so a slow or hung connection from the upstream MCP publisher release URL will stall the release workflow indefinitely with no timeout surfaced to operators or the release author.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher download/verify steps; release.yml mcp_verify job
findings.suggested_fix: curl -fsSL --max-time 120 --retry 3 --retry-delay 5 "<exact-version-url>" -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that evidence fragment upload steps use `if: always()` (so they run even after a step failure) and that the aggregation step explicitly lists expected fragment names and fails with a descriptive message if any are absent, rather than silently producing a partial file.
findings.finding: The evidence-consolidation aggregation step's partial-failure mode is unspecified: if any one of the three fragment upload steps (`evidence-shapin`, `evidence-build`, `evidence-mcp`) fails silently or is skipped (e.g., due to a preceding step failure with `if: always()` absent), the consolidation job will either skip the missing fragment silently or fail with a confusing download error — neither outcome is identified or handled in the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — release evidence AC; Scope — tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py check (f)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add pip-tools as an explicit install step or dev dependency (e.g. `pipx install pip-tools` in the docs runbook, or a `[dev]` extra entry in pyproject.toml) so the `pip-compile --generate-hashes` regeneration command named in AC3, the Scope, and docs/releasing.md is self-contained.
findings.finding: pip-tools (pip-compile) is named throughout the plan as the lock-file generation tool (Scope, AC3, docs AC grep check) but is absent from pyproject.toml dev/extras dependencies and no existing or proposed CI workflow step installs it — leaving the 'pip-compile --generate-hashes' regeneration command unrunnable without an unenumerated install step.
findings.location: Scope section (.github/release-requirements.txt) / AC3 / docs AC
findings.suggested_fix: In docs/releasing.md 'Pinning' section, add a one-time bootstrap line: `pipx install pip-tools` (mirroring the git-cliff install pattern at docs/releasing.md:91-93), so the regeneration command is self-contained for a maintainer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rationale statement (in the 'What' section's security contract description and/or a workflow comment) explaining that GitHub Actions internal artifact storage (`upload-artifact`/`download-artifact`) is within the trust boundary — the archive-level sha256sum in `mcp_verify` sufficiently binds the binary's identity because the artifact store is not externally writable — so re-verification in `mcp_registry` is deliberately omitted.
findings.finding: The plan's stated security guarantee — 'the OIDC-privileged job never downloads or holds an unverified binary' — relies on GitHub's internal artifact storage (`upload-artifact`/`download-artifact`) not mutating the extracted `mcp-publisher` binary between verification in `mcp_verify` and execution in `mcp_registry`. The archive digest is verified before extraction, but no digest of the extracted binary is computed and stored for re-assertion in `mcp_registry`. The plan never states this trust assumption, making the security model's boundary incomplete and difficult to audit.
findings.location: What section — 'Security contract — structural job split'; AC5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify GitHub Actions artifact storage tamper-resistance guarantees for the `mcp_verify` → `mcp_registry` handoff and, if artifact overwrite by concurrent jobs is possible, either add an explicit `actions: read` restriction on all non-`mcp_verify` jobs or re-verify the binary digest in `mcp_registry` before execution.
findings.finding: The two-job split treats GitHub Actions artifact storage (upload-artifact → download-artifact) as a trusted handoff channel for the verified `mcp-publisher` binary, but the plan does not verify or cite the tamper-resistance guarantees of this channel. In GitHub Actions, any job in the same workflow run that holds `actions: write` permission can upload an artifact under the same name, silently overwriting the verified binary before `mcp_registry` downloads it. The plan specifies per-job `permissions:` for `id-token:` and `contents:`, but never sets an explicit `actions:` permission on `mcp_registry` (or on the workflow-level default) to restrict artifact write access.
findings.location: What section: 'Security contract — structural job split' / AC line 5 / Scope: `mcp_verify` / `mcp_registry` jobs

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how pip-tools is installed before `pip-compile --generate-hashes` is run: either add `pip-tools` to `pyproject.toml` dev dependencies (so `pip install -e '.[dev]'` makes it available), or add an explicit install step (`pip install pip-tools`) to the `docs/releasing.md` pin-update procedure and to any CI step that regenerates the lock — and confirm the `--generate-hashes` flag name against `pip-compile --help`.
findings.finding: The plan mandates `pip-compile --generate-hashes` (pip-tools) as the canonical lock-file generation command in the Scope section, AC3, and the docs AC grep check, but pip-tools is not listed in `pyproject.toml` dev dependencies, is absent from any existing CI workflow install step, and is UNRESOLVED in the installed environment. No step in the proposed workflow or contributing instructions specifies how pip-tools is installed before the implementer can run `pip-compile --generate-hashes` to produce the initial `.github/release-requirements.txt`. This creates a bootstrapping gap: the lock file cannot be generated without pip-tools, but pip-tools has no declared install path.
findings.location: Scope section / AC3 (`pip-compile --generate-hashes --output-file=.github/release-requirements.txt .github/release-requirements.in`)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `pip-tools` to `pyproject.toml` under `[project.optional-dependencies]` dev (alongside the other dev tools), so that the existing `pip install -e '.[dev]'` step in gerrit-verify.yaml and test.yml automatically makes `pip-compile` available. Also add `pip install pip-tools` as a named step in the `docs/releasing.md` 'Pinning' section's lock-regeneration instructions.

findings.finding: The test suite verifies that the workflow YAML invokes `pip install --require-hashes` (static text check) but no test asserts that `.github/release-requirements.txt` itself actually contains `--hash=sha256:` entries. A developer could create a plain, hash-free requirements file; `test_release_workflow_pins.py` would still pass, but `pip install --require-hashes` would fail at real release time — a silent gap between the green test suite and a broken release.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — third bullet; Scope — `.github/release-requirements.txt`; `tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py`
findings.suggested_fix:
In `test_release_workflow_pins.py`, add a test such as:
```python
def test_release_requirements_contains_hashes():
    req = pathlib.Path('.github/release-requirements.txt').read_text()
    assert '--hash=sha256:' in req, (
        '.github/release-requirements.txt must be generated with '
        'pip-compile --generate-hashes; no hash entries found'
    )
```
This ensures the file was actually generated with `--generate-hashes` and that `--require-hashes` will not fail at release time due to a missing-hash file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `test_release_workflow_pins.py`'s SHA-pin and persist-credentials checks should extend `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` (the existing home for workflow structural assertions) rather than creating a parallel bespoke file, and document in the plan why `zizmor`/`actionlint` were evaluated and not adopted for the subset of checks those tools already cover.
findings.finding: The plan mandates two new bespoke test files (`test_release_workflow_pins.py`, `test_mcp_publisher_pin.py`) that implement raw YAML/grep static analysis of `release.yml`, rebuilding functionality the codebase already has in `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` (which reads workflow YAML files and asserts structural properties) and that established dedicated tools (`zizmor`, `actionlint`) already provide for SHA-pin and persist-credentials enforcement. The plan does not document why `zizmor` or extension of the existing parity test was considered and rejected.
findings.location: Scope section (`tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py` (new)); AC lines 1, 2, 4, 5, 6

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update docs/releasing.md 'Supply-chain attestations' section (line 45) to replace the mutable `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1` reference with the newly pinned SHA (with version comment), or explicitly assign ownership of that line update to this story's scope even if section prose ownership stays with another finding.
findings.finding: docs/releasing.md line 45 currently contains a mutable-tag reference ('pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1') in the existing 'Supply-chain attestations' section. The plan's scope restriction ('this story owns the Pinning section only') explicitly excludes updating this section. After the story SHA-pins that action in release.yml, docs/releasing.md:45 will still cite the old mutable tag as if it is the current configuration — a verifiable incoherence the plan's own section-ownership exclusion leaves in place.
findings.location: Scope section — 'docs/releasing.md — … Section ownership: this story owns the "Pinning" section only'; docs/releasing.md line 45
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) broaden this story's docs/releasing.md scope to include updating the mutable-tag citation at line 45 when the SHA-pin is applied, or (b) add a note in the plan that the 'Supply-chain attestations' section update is the explicit responsibility of whichever concurrent finding owns release.yml's publish step, with a cross-reference so it is not dropped.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/releasing.md` line 45 (Supply-chain attestations section) to the edit scope, updating the `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1` reference to the pinned SHA format (or add an explicit 'out of scope — will not update' rationale).
findings.finding: docs/releasing.md line 45 contains a mutable-tag reference ('pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1') in the existing 'Supply-chain attestations' section. The plan's Scope restricts its docs edits to a new 'Pinning' section only, so this reference is an unenumerated edit target that will remain stale after release.yml is SHA-pinned — the docs will describe the action with a mutable tag while the workflow uses a commit SHA.
findings.location: Scope section (docs/releasing.md ownership) / docs/releasing.md line 45
findings.suggested_fix: Either expand the docs/releasing.md scope to update line 45 alongside the Pinning section, or add an explicit note in the Scope that the Supply-chain attestations section's existing mutable-tag reference is intentionally left as a human-readable label.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider and explicitly reject or adopt `zizmor` (and/or `actionlint`) as a pre-commit hook for SHA-pin and persist-credentials enforcement before committing to a bespoke `test_release_workflow_pins.py` grep implementation — document the rationale in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan mandates bespoke YAML-grep logic in `test_release_workflow_pins.py` to enforce full-SHA `uses:`, `persist-credentials: false`, and `--no-isolation` without considering or rejecting established prior art: `zizmor` (GitHub's own recommended Actions security linter with explicit SHA-pin and persist-credentials rules) and `actionlint` (static type/lint for workflow YAML). Both tools are commonly integrated via pre-commit — the same hook runner the project already runs in CI (`pre-commit run --all-files` is a shared gate in `gerrit-verify.yaml` and `test.yml`). Choosing a bespoke grep test over these tools is a novel architectural choice that requires justification, which the plan does not provide.
findings.location: What section / Scope: `tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py` / AC lines 1–2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `zizmor` pre-commit hook (e.g. `repo: https://github.com/woodruffw/zizmor-action` or via `pip install zizmor`) to `.pre-commit-config.yaml`; its `unpinned-uses` and `persist-credentials` rules cover ACs 1 and 2 with a maintained parser. The bespoke `test_release_workflow_pins.py` can then focus on project-specific assertions (`--no-isolation`, evidence fragment names, job-permission structure) that `zizmor` does not cover.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the expected OIDC token audience for the `mcp_registry` job (e.g., specify `--aud <mcp-registry-audience>` if `mcp-publisher login` accepts it, or document that the MCP registry requires the default GitHub audience and why that is acceptable) so the access posture of the `id-token: write` boundary-crossing call is explicit.
findings.finding: The restructured `mcp_registry` job holds `id-token: write` and calls `mcp-publisher login`, but the plan never declares the expected OIDC token audience (`--aud` / `audience` parameter) for that call. In GitHub Actions, `id-token: write` mints a JWT whose `aud` claim defaults to the generic `https://github.com/` audience unless explicitly narrowed; the MCP registry login command likely accepts any GitHub-issued OIDC token, and a broad audience means the minted token could be replayed at any other service that trusts the same audience. This is an undeclared access posture on a privileged boundary-crossing path.
findings.location: What / MCP publisher section — 'mcp_registry' job definition; Scope — `.github/workflows/release.yml`; AC item for MCP publisher (5th AC)
findings.suggested_fix: In the `mcp_registry` job definition and in the `docs/releasing.md` Pinning section, add a comment/prose declaring the OIDC audience in use (e.g., `# OIDC audience: <value> — narrowed to MCP registry per mcp-publisher --aud flag` or, if the tool does not support audience narrowing, `# OIDC audience: default https://github.com/ — MCP registry requires this; no narrowing available`). Add an assertion to `test_release_workflow_pins.py` that the `mcp_registry` job either sets a non-default OIDC audience or contains an explicit rationale comment so the posture is never silently widened.

findings.finding: The cutover to `--no-isolation` + hash-locked build environment is proven only by a static text-presence check (`test_release_workflow_pins.py` asserting the string `--no-isolation` appears in `release.yml`). No AC or step exercises the actual `pip install --require-hashes … && python -m build --no-isolation` code path end-to-end, so the lock file could be incomplete, the hashes could be wrong, or the build could fail in ways that are invisible pre-merge.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — third bullet (hash-locked build tools) and fourth bullet (--no-isolation enforcement)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Makefile target `test-release-build` that: (1) creates a temp venv, (2) runs `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r .github/release-requirements.txt`, (3) runs `python -m build --no-isolation --outdir /tmp/test-dist`, and (4) asserts the expected wheel and sdist appear. Add an AC: 'Running `make test-release-build` succeeds — the lock file is complete, hashes are valid, and `--no-isolation` build produces a wheel and sdist.'

findings.finding: The docs/releasing.md AC specifies 'a grep for the SHA-pin rationale heading' as one of the three required substrings verified by `test_release_workflow_pins.py`, but does not state the exact string/heading that the grep must match. The test implementer must invent the heading text, and there is no guarantee the docs author uses the same string the test expects.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — sixth bullet (docs/releasing.md), specifically item (a)/(c) of the three-grep assertion
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a grep for the SHA-pin rationale heading' with the concrete command: `grep -q '## Pinning' docs/releasing.md` (or whichever heading the plan mandates for the section), consistent with the plan's instruction that the section is named 'Pinning'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the new `mcp_registry` job retains `needs: [publish, mcp_verify]` (not just `needs: mcp_verify`) so it is still skipped immediately when `publish` fails, and document the dual-needs relationship explicitly in the Scope / What sections.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `mcp_registry needs: mcp_verify` but silently drops the existing `needs: publish` relationship from the current `mcp_registry` job (release.yml line 111). After the job split, if `publish` fails, `mcp_verify` still runs and succeeds (it only fetches a binary), then `mcp_registry` starts (its only declared dependency is `mcp_verify`), attempts `mcp-publisher login` and then `mcp-publisher publish` against a version that doesn't exist on PyPI, and spins the 5-minute polling loop before failing — instead of being skipped immediately as it is today. This changes the failure mode from a clean fast-skip to a 5-minute wasted wait.
findings.location: What section — 'Security contract — structural job split'; Scope section — release.yml bullet
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope and What sections, replace 'mcp_registry (`needs: mcp_verify`)' with 'mcp_registry (`needs: [publish, mcp_verify]`)'. The `mcp_verify` job itself has no prerequisite beyond `build` completing (the binary fetch is independent of publishing), so its `needs:` should be `needs: build` or omitted if it can run immediately.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default contents of `.github/release-requirements.in` explicitly in the Scope section — at minimum list `build`, `hatchling`, `twine` as the required entries and either name any additional validation tools (e.g., `pip-audit`) or explicitly state that no others are included by default.
findings.finding: The `.github/release-requirements.in` input file contents are only partially specified: `build`, Hatchling, and Twine are named but 'any artifact-validation tools' is left vague. An executor must decide which tools to include, making the lock file output non-deterministic across implementers and creating a potential 'choose an appropriate X' gap.
findings.location: Scope — '.github/release-requirements.txt (new)'; What — 'Create a reviewed release lock/constraints file covering build, Hatchling, Twine, any artifact-validation tools'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope bullet: 'Default `.github/release-requirements.in` contents: `build`, `hatchling`, `twine` (no additional artifact-validation tools unless a specific tool is named in a follow-on story).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/emergency-release procedure to `docs/releasing.md` (and reference it from the plan's Scope section) describing how to recover when the hash-lock install or mcp_verify artifact handoff blocks a release — e.g., 'regenerate lock with pip-compile and fast-track through Gerrit' or a documented bypass with required approvals.
findings.finding: The plan makes release.yml's install step non-recoverable during a production release if the hash-lock file is stale or corrupted (`pip install --require-hashes` fails hard), and introduces a new inter-job artifact handoff that can fail. No rollback/back-out plan is described for a situation where these new gates block a time-sensitive release. T4 requires that a destructive/irreversible step include an explicit rollback mechanism, not just acknowledgement.
findings.location: What section (hash-locked install), Scope ('.github/release-requirements.txt'), AC #3 ('fails the build if any package was replaced or its hash doesn't match')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Emergency release procedure' subsection to docs/releasing.md specifying: (1) how to regenerate release-requirements.txt quickly (the pip-compile command), (2) the minimum Gerrit approval path for an urgent lock-update, and (3) what to do if the mcp_verify/mcp_registry artifact handoff fails (re-run the verify job, check artifact retention settings). Reference this from the plan Scope section so it has a covering AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'Why these constraints exist' sub-section (or an ADR entry) to `docs/releasing.md` documenting (a) why `--no-isolation` is mandatory for Hatchling version enforcement and (b) why `mcp_verify`/`mcp_registry` must remain separate jobs (OIDC token reachability from step 1 of any job), and extend AC (f) or add a 4th literal-substring assertion in `test_release_workflow_pins.py` (e.g. `no-isolation` and `id-token: write`) to ensure this rationale survives future doc edits.
findings.finding: The `--no-isolation` build strategy and the `mcp_verify`/`mcp_registry` job-split security model are novel architectural decisions with non-obvious security rationale, but the plan does not require capturing either decision in an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or a dedicated `docs/releasing.md` sub-section — only three literal substrings (`pip-compile --generate-hashes`, `MCP publisher`, `full-SHA`) are asserted. A future contributor could silently revert `--no-isolation` (restoring the free-resolution path) or collapse the job split (re-colocating an unverified binary with the OIDC token) without any guidance explaining why these choices were made.
findings.location: Scope → `docs/releasing.md` / AC: 'docs/releasing.md contains a Pinning section covering…' / What → '–no-isolation' and 'Security contract — structural job split'
findings.suggested_fix: In the `docs/releasing.md` 'Pinning' section add a '## Security invariants' block with two numbered notes: (1) '`--no-isolation` is required: removing it bypasses the hash-locked env and allows Hatchling to re-resolve freely.' (2) 'The `mcp_verify` and `mcp_registry` jobs must remain separate: GitHub Actions exposes the OIDC token endpoint from the first step of any job holding `id-token: write`; co-locating download with publish re-introduces the supply-chain risk this split was designed to eliminate.' Add `no-isolation` and `mcp_verify` as two additional literal-substring assertions in `test_release_workflow_pins.py` (AC (d)) so a doc edit that removes either note fails `make test`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a contingency note to `docs/releasing.md` (Pinning section) describing what to do when the pinned MCP publisher URL is unavailable (e.g., re-pin to the next available version, or document a manual override procedure), so operators have a runbook rather than an undocumented block.
findings.finding: The plan specifies pinning the MCP publisher to an exact upstream release URL but provides no guidance on what to do when that exact-version URL becomes permanently unavailable (e.g., the upstream release is deleted or yanked), leaving the release process fully blocked with no degraded-mode or contingency path documented.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher pin; Scope — release.yml mcp_verify job; docs/releasing.md Pinning section

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
